coredata.dc:title: Selective and sensitive electrochemical detection of doxorubicin via a novel magnesium oxide/carbon dot nanocomposite based sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Abhishek Singh, Th.
Sharma, Vinit
Thakur, Neelam
Tejwan, Neeraj
Sharma, Anirudh
Das, Joydeep
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, we prepared novel and multifunctional carbon dots/magnesium oxide (CDs/MgO) nanocomposites with varied amount of CDs (0.5–5 wt%) via a facile hydrothermal process. The nanocomposites were then utilized for the fabrication of CDs/MgO-modified screen printed carbon electrodes (CDs/MgO/SPCE) for highly sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) using cyclic voltammetry. All the prepared CDs/MgO nanocomposites possess excellent efficiency towards the electrochemical oxidation of the DOX at the surface of SPCE by facilitating electron transfer reaction with increasing concentration of CDs in comparison with bare MgO nanoparticles (NPs) and CDs due to increased electroactive surface area. Besides, the oxidation peak current was also found to be increased with increasing scan rate from 50 to 150 mV/s−1. The CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite exhibited maximum oxidation response for the detection of DOX (10 µM) at pH 5 and at a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1. Under the optimized conditions, CDs-5.0/MgO modified SPCE exhibited a linear response for the determination of DOX in the concentration range of 0.1–1 µM (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.99) with a low detection limit of 0.09 µM. Furthermore, a negligible effect of the common interfering agents validated the superior selectivity of the sensor (CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE) towards DOX. The suitable linear range, low detection limit, rapid response, and excellent sensitivity of the proposed sensor (CDs/MgO/SPCE) make it a promising tool for the determination of DOX in biological, clinical, and pharmaceutical fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MgONPs
Carbon dots
CDs/MgO Nanocomposite
Cyclic voltammetry
DOX Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the CDs/MgO-modified screen printed carbon electrode (CDs/MgO /SPCE)
Electrochemical analysis of the modified CDs/MgO/SPCE
Electrochemical behaviour of DOX modified CDs/MgO/SPCE surface
Optimization of concentrations of CDs-5.0/MgO for electrode modification
Effect of pH on the electrochemical behavior of DOX
Effect of scan rate on the determination of DOX
Effect of different concentrations of DOX at the modified SPCE and determination of LOD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Shoolini University, Solan, H.P. for providing instrumental facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the preparation and electrocatalytic detection of DOX via CDs/MgO-modified SPCE.

(a) HRTEM image of synthesized CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite (b) Size Distribution graph of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite. (d) XRD patterns of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite. (e) TGA analysis of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) Raman spectra, and (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of nanocomposites.
(a) CV and (b) Nyquist plots of the MgO/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/MgO/SPCE, CDs-1.0/MgO/SPCE, CDs-2.5/MgO/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE in the presence of 1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] solution.

(a) Cyclic voltammograms of 10 µM DOX at different modified SPCE surfaces in 0.1 M PBS (pH 5.0) with a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. (b) Optimization of DOX oxidation peak current by modification of SPCE with different concentrations of CDs-5.0/MgO.
(a) Influence of pH on anodic peak current response of DOX at modified CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE. (b) Plot of the Ipa (anodic peak current) vs pH and (c) Epa (anodic peak potential) vs pH.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms on CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE surface at different scan rates ranging from 50 to 150 mV s−1. (b) log Ipa vs log scan rate.
(a) Cyclic voltammograms of DOX with different concentrations (0.1–10 µM) at modified CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE. (b) Plot of Ipa (anodic peak current) vs DOX concentration.
CV response of 10 μM DOX at CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE surface in the presence and absence of interfering agents.
Electroactive surface area of various modified electrodes.
Comparison of the proposed electrochemical sensor with other modified electrodes for the determination of DOX.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: rGO/Au/Ppy, reduced graphene oxide/gold/polypyrrole; p-AgSAE, polished silver solid amalgam electrode; HMDE, hanging mercury drop electrode; BPPDNi/Pt:CO-NPs, bis(1,10-phenanthroline)(1,10-phenanthroline-5,6-dione) nickel(II) hexafluorophosphate/Pt:Co nanoparticles; LDG, low-defect graphene; PARG, poly-arginine; GCE, glassy carbon electrode; l-GSH–MoS2–CYS, l-glutathione- MoS2-cystamine; SDS, sodium dodecyl sulfate; MCPE, modified carbon paste electrode; CDs/MgO/SPCE, Carbon dots/magnesium oxide/ screen printed carbon electrode; CV, Cyclic voltammetry; DPV, differential pulse voltammetry; DPCSV, differential pulse cathodic stripping voltammetry; SWV, square wave voltammetry; CPE, carbon paste electrode.

head.ce:title: Selective and sensitive electrochemical detection of doxorubicin via a novel magnesium oxide/carbon dot nanocomposite based sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Advanced Chemical Sciences, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan, Himachal Pradesh 173229, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Advanced Chemical Sciences, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Shoolini University, Solan, Himachal Pradesh 173229, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl 796004, Mizoram, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl 796004, Mizoram, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Mizoram University, Aizawl 796004, Mizoram, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, we prepared novel and multifunctional carbon dots/magnesium oxide (CDs/MgO) nanocomposites with varied amount of CDs (0.5–5 wt%) via a facile hydrothermal process. The nanocomposites were then utilized for the fabrication of CDs/MgO-modified screen printed carbon electrodes (CDs/MgO/SPCE) for highly sensitive and selective electrochemical detection of the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) using cyclic voltammetry. All the prepared CDs/MgO nanocomposites possess excellent efficiency towards the electrochemical oxidation of the DOX at the surface of SPCE by facilitating electron transfer reaction with increasing concentration of CDs in comparison with bare MgO nanoparticles (NPs) and CDs due to increased electroactive surface area. Besides, the oxidation peak current was also found to be increased with increasing scan rate from 50 to 150 mV/s−1. The CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite exhibited maximum oxidation response for the detection of DOX (10 µM) at pH 5 and at a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1. Under the optimized conditions, CDs-5.0/MgO modified SPCE exhibited a linear response for the determination of DOX in the concentration range of 0.1–1 µM (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.99) with a low detection limit of 0.09 µM. Furthermore, a negligible effect of the common interfering agents validated the superior selectivity of the sensor (CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE) towards DOX. The suitable linear range, low detection limit, rapid response, and excellent sensitivity of the proposed sensor (CDs/MgO/SPCE) make it a promising tool for the determination of DOX in biological, clinical, and pharmaceutical fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, several types of analytical techniques such as UV–vis spectrophotometry, ratiometric fluorescent probes, mass spectrometry, chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, etc. are utilized for the detection of a number of analytes such as signal mediators Catecholamines (CAs), metal ions, amino acids [1–4], contaminants of emerging concern (pharmaceuticals, personal care products, pesticides, nanaomaterials, surfactants, endocrine disruptors, etc.), [5], toxic elements (As, Sb) [6] and organic contaminants [7] in biological and environmental analysis. In addition, various electroanalytical techniques such as differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), square-wave voltammetry (SWV), cyclic voltammetry (CV), amperometry and potentiometry are also being employed for the detection purpose of several antiviral, antimalarial and anticancer drug molecules in pharmaceutical formulation and biological samples [8,9], as well as numerous hazardous heavy metals and harmful pollutants (chemical or biological contaminants) in sea or waste water [10,11]. The voltammetric techniques have however received considerable attention nowadays for the biological and environmental analysis purpose due to their high sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility, lass sampling time and risk of contamination, low cost, and easy handling [11].

Recently, bioelectrocatalysis has been explored by the scientific community where enzymatic biocatalysts (commonly known as enzyme electrodes) accelerate the electrochemical reactions in biosensors and biofuel cells [12]. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) is a highly efficient anticancer drug which is used as first-line treatment for a variety of cancers. However, accurate detection of DOX level in patients is very important in order to reduce its side effect for improved therapeutic outcomes. Although various analytical techniques for DOX sensing have been reported, but those techniques are very costly and less sensitive, as well as require long sampling time for detection; therefore, electroanalytical methods have received considerable attention for the analysis of DOX [13]. The presence of an electroactive quinone-hydroquinone redox system in DOX allows for its easy and highly sensitive electroanalytical determination [14]. However, DOX exhibits very slow electron transfer rate at an unmodified electrode surface. Therefore, various chemically modified electrodes with modifier nanoparticles are used for the electroanalytical detection of DOX [15]. In comparison to conventional electrodes, the screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) is highly effective for electrochemical detection of analytes because it can easily be modified and has a low background current and wide potential range due to its rough and porous structure. These electrodes also have a number of attractive features, including excellent conductivity, high chemical stability, good reproducibility, good connectivity, quick response, and low power requirements [16,17].

Several kinds of metals and their oxides, as well as carbon nanomaterials have recently been used as modifier nanomaterials for the fabrication of electrochemical sensors [18]. Besides, nanocomposites sometime offer unusual properties of the parent nanomaterials and show remarkable potential to be used in numerous sensor fabrications. Carbon nano or micro-particles are also attracting much interest of researchers due to their intrinsic low toxicity, high solubility in a variety of solvents, excellent electronic properties, excellent conductivity, large specific surface area, high biocompatibility, low cost of production, and facile surface modification [19]. Due to these distinctive properties, carbon materials have widely been used as modifier in the fabrication of electrochemical sensors to improve the rate of electron transferring processin several chemical and biological sensing applications [20–23]. Besides, magnesium oxide nanoparticles (MgONPs) are emerging as a new class of nanomaterials because of their large ionic character, high isoelectric stability [24], wide band gap, excellent electronic conductivity, non toxicity, simple stoichiometry and surface structural defects [25]. It can easily be synthesized with a wide range of structure as well as size from low cost starting materials; and used in several catalysis and sensing [26] applications. MgONPs are reported to be used as sensing materials for the electrochemical detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, glucose, uric acid and H2O2 [27,28] at room temperature with low detection limit and high reproducibility [29].

Motivated by these superior electrocatalytic properties of both carbon materials and MgONPs; in this study, we used a facile hydrothermal process to fabricate a novel CDs/MgO nanocomposite, which was subsequently used to modify screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) for the volatammetric detection of DOX with high selectivity and sensitivity. However, this combination has never been used for the voltammetric detection of DOX so far, although MgONPs decorated CDs have been reported to be used for H2S gas sensing at a low operating temperature with high selectivity and sensitivity [30].

Magnesium (II) nitrate, Concentrated HCl, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), were purchased from Loba Chemie (Mumbai, India). CDs were prepared from Red Korean ginseng (RKG) which was obtained from amazon.in. KH2PO4 and K2HPO4 were procured from Himedia (Mumbai, India). Doxorubicin hydrochloride was procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Pure double distilled water was used for the preparation of buffer and DOX solution.

The MgO nanoparticles were synthesized using a microwave-assisted heating technique. In 10 mL distilled water, 2 g magnesium nitrate and 5 g sodium hydroxide were added and mixed under stirring condition for 0.5 h [31,32]. The obtained precipitate was then washed and oven dried at 50 °C. To obtain MgO nanoparticles, the sample was heated in a domestic microoven (480 W, IFB, India) for 15 min followed by calcination at 400 °C for 5 h inside a furnace.

Aqueous root extract of red Korean Ginseng (RKG) was utilized to prepare CDs via microwave irradiation technique using a domestic microwave oven (480 W, IFB, India). The CDs were prepared by 7 consecutive heating cycles for 2 min with 2 min cooling interval in each cycle. The resulting CDs were then purified by suspending them in water, dialysis with a 3500 Da MWCO membrane, and freeze dried.

A one-step hydrothermal technique was employed to make CDs/MgO nanohybrid [33]. At first, 1 g of MgONPs was suspended in 10 mL of deionized water, and after that, various amounts of CDs (5–50 mg) were added to the solution. A homogeneous mixture was then prepared by ultrasonication (ACZET, Gujrat, India) for 0.5 h under stirring condition. The solution was heated thereafter for 3 h at 100 °C in a hydrothermal reactor (Fermex solutions llp, Mohali, india). The product was then obtained after centrifugation (Remi, Vasai, India) at 5,000 rpm for 10 min, washed with water and freeze-dried. The prepared samples were designated as CDs (0.5–5.0)/MgO based on the weight percentage of CDs (0.5–5.0 wt%) used in the synthesis process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the CDs/MgO-modified screen printed carbon electrode (CDs/MgO /SPCE)
ce:section.ce:para: At first different suspension of the nanocomposites were prepared for electrode modification by dissolving 4 mg of the CDs/MgO nanohybrids in 2 mL acetone containing nafion as binder under ullrasonic condition for 30 min. The different suspensions were then applied as droplets on SPCE electrode, and dried at room temperature. The prepared SPCEs were represented as CDs-(0.5–5.0)/CeO2/SPCE which corresponds to 0.5–5.0 wt% of CDs.

The structural properties of the nanocomposites were studied by XRD pattern using X’ Pert Pro, Panalytical, Netherlands with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The size of MgO-CDs nanohybrid was examined by Transmission electron microscope (TEM) of JEM-2100 plus, Japan. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Nexsa base; Thermo fisher scientific) was used to evaluate the elemental composition of the samples. The UV–vis spectrophotometer of Wensar (LMSP-UV1900; Chennai, India)was used to record the UV–visible spectra of CDs/MgO nanocomposite. The functional groups were studied by using FTIR instrument (L1600312; CA, USA) of Agilent Technologies. The Raman spectra was taken using a Raman spectrometer (Xplora Plus, Horiba, Japan). All electrochemical tests were carried out on the FRA-2 μAUTOLAB TYPE III by using a standard three electrode system: (CDs/MgO/SPCE) as a working electrode; Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and carbon as a counter electrode.

As a supporting electrolyte, all electrochemical tests were carried out in 0.1 M Phosphate Buffer Solution (PBS). Cyclic voltammetry (CV) (FRA2 µAutolab type iii, Metrohm, (New Dehli, India) technique was employed for the determination of DOX in our present study.
Here we have first prepared MgONPs as well as CDs separately, and then CDs/MgO nanocomposites were fabricated for the determination of doxorubicin electrochemically (Fig. 1 ).

The shape as well as size of CDs-5.0/MgOnanocomosite were examined using HRTEM (Fig. 2 a), which revealed irregular shape of the nanocomposite with size ranging from 20 to 100 nm (Fig. 2b), XPS analysis was done to check the elemental composition of the CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposites (Fig. 2c). Mg(1 s), O(1 s) and C(1 s) showed their binding energy peaks at 1303.9, 531.96 and 285.96 eV respectively [34,35], thereby confirming the successful combination of CDs with MgONPs. The crystalline structure of CDs-5.0/MgO nanohybrid was checked by powered XRD analysis. The (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes were observed as high intensity and sharp diffraction peaks at the corresponding 2θvalues of 37.94°, 43.09°, 62.3°, 74.70°, and 78.66° (Fig. 2d). All these planes correspond to the face centered cubic nature of MgONPs [36]. FTIR analysis was performed to check the chemical nature of the nanomaterials. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) also revealed good thermal stability of the nanocomposite (Fig. 2e) [37]. The types of functional groups present in CDs, MgONPs and CD/MgO nanocomposites were studied by FTIR analysis. The band between 500 and 860 cm−1 was obtained because of Mg-O bending vibrations (Fig. 3 a). The bands at 3461 cm−1 and 1648 cm−1 were obtained due to water adsorption [38]. The CDs showed a broad band centered at 3435 cm−1 and peak at 2926 cm−1 because of OH (from phenolic and carboxylic acid groups) as well as CH groups respectively. The presence of aromatic CC and CO bonds was also evident from the observed peaks at 1413 cm−1and 1627 cm−1 respectively. The peak at 1413 cm−1 was observed due to the CH bending vibration of CH2 or CH3 groups. The symmetric vibration of COC bonds might be responsible for the peak at 1026 cm−1. All the CD/MgO nanocompopsites showed similar characteristic FTIR peaks of both CD and MgO. However, the intensity of CO stretching decreased significantly in the nanocomposites due to their strong interaction with Mg. [39].

The functional group analysis was also done by Raman spectroscopic analysis. The characteristic peaks of both MgO and CD were clearly seen in the Raman spectra of the CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite (Fig. 3b). MgONPs showed peaks at 1321 cm−1 (D) and 1546 cm−1 (G) [40,41], whereas CDs showed the corresponding D and G bands at 1338 cm−1 (D) and 1546 cm−1 (G) respectively. Here, the G bands of both MgO and CD are overlapped. Furthermore, D1, D2 and D3 peaks at 1224, 1238 and 1436 cm−1 were also observed in CDs, and might be assigned to COOH/C-OH functional groups [42,43].

UV–vis spectroscopy was used to explore the optical characteristics of the prepared nanoparticles. The absorption spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. 3c. The unique absorption peak centred at 230 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic carbon carbon double bonds, and peaks at 282 and 337 nm can be due to the π-π* as well as n-π* transition of the carbon oxygen double bonds [44]. Pure MgONPs displayed an absorption peak at 230 nm [45]. The UV–vis absorption nature of the CDs/MgO nanocomposites were also studied, and absorption peaks similar to MgONPs were observed at ∼209–227 nm. As the concentration of CD increased, a gradual increase in absorption intensity was observed, and maximum absorbance was observed for CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis of the modified CDs/MgO/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, the electrodes were further characterized by measuring the surface area andcharge resistance of each modified SPCE in the presence of a 1 mM potassium ferricyanide, K3[Fe(CN)6]. The cyclic voltammograms of all the modified SPCEs are shown in Fig. 4 a, which showed well-defined redox peaks within the potential range −0.5 to 0.5 V. The peak current values were increased with increasing the amount of CDs, and the highest peak current was obtained for the CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE sensor as compared to the other hybrid (CDs-2.5/MgO/SPCE, CDs-1.0/MgO/SPCE, CDs-0.5/MgO/SPCE)as well as bare (CDs/SPCE, and MgO/SPCE)electrodes. Furthermore, the Randles-Sevcik equation was used to determine the dynamic surface area of all the modified electrodes [46]; Ip = (2.69 × 105) n3
/
2 A D1
/
2 ν 1
/
2C. (where Ip is peak current, n is the number of electrons involved in the reaction i.e., equal to 1, A is effective area of electrode surface (cm2), D is diffusion coefficient (for K3[Fe(CN)6] value is 7.6 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), ν is scan rate (Vs−1), C is concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6] (10−6 mol cm−3). The effective surface area values for MgO/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/MgO/SPCE, CDs-1.0/MgO/SPCE, CDs-2.5/MgO/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE were calculated to be 0.021, 0.024, 0.026, 0.033, 0.035 and 0.042 cm2, respectively (Table 1 ). The observed results indicated that the high electrocatalytic behaviour of all modified SPCEs were due to their fast electron transfer rate and large electro-active surface area.

Here we have also employed electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to check the electrocatalytic behaviour of the nanomaterials [36]. In the presence of 1 mM K3[FeCN6] solution as redox probe, the acquired Nyquist plots for the different modified SPCE are shown in Fig. 4b. Among all the prepared nanomaterials, MgO/SPCE showed the largest arc radius, whereas as CDs/SPCE showed lesser arc radius in the Nyquist curve. However, the arc radius was decreased with increasing amount of CDs in the nanocomposites, and the smallest arc radius was observed for CDs-2.5/MgO/SPCE and CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE, thereby suggesting the reduced charge transfer resistance for these two electrodes. With these low charge transfer resistance and high electron transfer rate, CDs-2.5/MgO/SPCE and CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE can be assumed to exhibit superior electrochemical performance via the combined/synergistic electrocatalytic effects of MgO and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviour of DOX modified CDs/MgO/SPCE surface
ce:section.ce:para: In this present study we conducted cyclic voltammetric studies for DOX determination on the modified CDs/MgO/SPCE surfaces. At a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1, the electrochemical behaviour of DOX (10 µM) was examined in 0.1 (M) PBS at pH 5.0, and we observed a pair of redox peaks in the potential range of –0.6 to 0.6 V with all the modified SPCE in the corresponding cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 5 a). The peak current response of DOX at the bare SPCE is essentially negligible, whereas all the modified SPCE showed better peak currents, thereby indicating efficient electrocatalysis of DOX via increased electron transfer rate. We also observed that the electrodes modified with both CDs and MgONPs showed higher electrocatalytic efficiency as compared with bare CDs and MgONPs modified electrodes, and the efficiency was further increased with increasing amount of CDs in the nanocomposites. The superior electrocatalytic activity of the CDs/MgONPs dual-modified SPCE is attributed to the increase in surface area as well as active sites for efficient electron transfer and higher electrical conductivity by the combined effect of both CDs and MgONPs [47]. Besides, efficient attachment of DOX on the CDs/MgO/SPCE surface via the π–π stacking interaction with CDs may also be responsible for improved catalyric efficiency of the electrodes towards DOX [48]. The anodic peak current obtained with CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE was found to be the highest among all the modified SPCE. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs played a very important role in improving the electrocatalytic behavior of MgONPs. The CDs with polar oxygenated functional groups facilitates DOX attachment via ionic or hydrogen bonding, while the sp2-C rich surface assists electron transfer [49], thereby improving the DOX reactivity and signal responsiveness at the modified CDs/MgO/SPCE surface. We have conducted all other electrochemical experiments with CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE as it showed the highest signal response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of concentrations of CDs-5.0/MgO for electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: The concentrations of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposites were optimized to improve the sensitivity of the electrodes for DOX sensing. In the present study, six separate suspensions of 5.0 CDs/MgO were prepared in 2 mL acetone by adding 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 mg of 5.0 CDs/MgO followed by sonication for 30 min. The amount of CDs-5.0/MgO casted on SPCE significantly influenced the redox peak current (Fig. 5b). With the increase in concentration of CDs-5.0/MgO nanocomposites from 1 to 4 mg/2 mL, the anodic peak current was first increased; thereafter the peak current was gradually decreased with increasing the concentrations from 5 to 6 mg/ 2 mL. The nanocomposite concentration of 4 mg/2 mL acetone is chosen as optimum for further experiments. The drop in peak current value is most likely owing to an increase in thickness of the CDs/MgO coating on the electrode surface, thereby reducing the interaction between the electrode surface and DOX [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the electrochemical behavior of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Another crucial factor that influences the peak current of the analyte is pH of the supporting electrolyte (0.1 M PBS). In this study, we tested the effect of pH (3,4,5,6 & 7) towards the electrocatalytic behaviour of 10.0 µM DOX using CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE (Fig. 6 a). The results showed that when the pH value increased from 3 to 5, the DOX oxidation/anodic peak current (Ipa) also increased. However, the anodic peak current was further decreased with increasing the pH value upto 7, therefore pH 5 was taken as optimum for further experiments (Fig. 6b). We have also observed a shift in anodic peak potentials (Epa) to a lower value with increasing pH, therefore suggesting that proton transfer plays an important role in the oxidation reaction of DOX. The variation of the anodic peak potential with pH is shown in (Fig. 6c) and the corresponding linear regression equation could be expressed as Epa (V) = −0.035x + 0.4883 (R2 = 0.99). This calculated slope value is close to the Nernst slope (0.05 V pH−1), thereby indicating that the redox reaction of DOX on the modified electrode surface could be mediated by the 2e−/2H+ mechanism [50,51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate on the determination of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: CV was used to investigate the effect of scan rate (50–150 mV s−1) on the oxidation peak current of 10 μM DOX at the surface of CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE to check their electro-oxidation mechanism (Fig. 7 a).The results showed that an increase in the scan rate caused the oxidation peak current to increase. The linear calibration plot between anodic peak current (log Ipa) and scan rate (log ν) is shown in Fig. 7b; log Ipa = 0.83005 log ν − 0.9447 (R2 = 0.99) which showed that the electrochemical oxidation of DOX is anadsorption-controlled process.In general; if the obtained slope is close to 1.0, it is assumed to be adsorption-controlled electrode process. On the other hand, if the slope value is close to 0.5, it is diffusion-controlled process [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different concentrations of DOX at the modified SPCE and determination of LOD
ce:section.ce:para: Here we also summarized the comparison of the LOD values of DOX determined with different types of hybrid electrodes [53–60]. The observed LOD of our modified SPCE is comparable or even better than the previously published literature values (Table 3 ). Therefore, this novel CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE can be used for highly efficient electrochemical determination of DOX in real samples.

In this study, the various interfering agents were used to evaluate the selectivity of the synthesized electrochemical sensor (CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE) towards DOX. The oxidation peak current values of DOX (10 μM)were recorded in the presence of 100 μM ascorbic acid, sucrose, urea, folic acid,and glucose. The current response of DOX was shown to be not significantly affected by these interfering agents up to a 10-fold higher concentration (Fig. 9 ). These results proved the superior selectivity ofCDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE sensor towards DOX.

In this current study we have reported the development of a highly efficient electrochemical sensing platform based on CDs/MgO/SPCE for the determination of doxorubicin using cyclic voltammetry methods. The CDs/MgO nanocomposites showedimproved electrocatalytic performances towards DOX as compared with bare CDs and MgO when used as electrode modifier. The 5 wt% CDs containing nanocomposite-modifed SPCE (CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE) displayed the highest anodic peak current with a LOD of 0.09 µM.at pH 5 and at a scan rate of 50 mV/s−1. Additionally, the modifed SPCE(CDs-5.0/MgO/SPCE) showed excellent selectivity in the presence of common interfering agents. All these results clearly demonstrated that the CDs-5.0/MgO-modified SPCE can be used for the determination of DOX in biological, clinical, and pharmaceutical samples.

Th. Abhishek Singh: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Vinit Sharma: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Neelam Thakur: Formal analysis, Validation, Software. Neeraj Tejwan: Formal analysis, Validation, Software. Anirudh Sharma: Formal analysis, Validation, Software. Joydeep Das: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Shoolini University, Solan, H.P. for providing instrumental facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analytical approaches to determination of carnitine in biological materials, foods and dietary supplements
